summary: Jeff Atwood responds to alarming headlines about GPU-accelerated password cracking by doing the actual math. While a $150 graphics card can crack an 8-character alphabetic password in 3 days instead of 2 months, adding just 4 more characters extends crack time to nearly 62,000 years. He demonstrates that password length, not complexity, is the critical factor in security, and argues that brute force attacks remain fundamentally ineffective against reasonably-sized passwords. The post advocates for passphrases over traditional passwords as an easy, memorable way to achieve security through length.
key_points: GPU-assisted cracking is 20x faster than CPU-based methods, but this speed increase is easily neutralized by adding a few characters to your password
An 8-character password takes 3 days to crack; a 12-character password takes 62,000 years with the same hardware
Password length matters far more than character complexity—even all-lowercase 14-character passphrases are essentially uncrackable by brute force
Brute force attacks are inefficient compared to social engineering and rainbow table attacks
Passphrases are both more secure and easier to remember than complex short passwords
money_quotes: For those of you keeping score at home, with a 12 character password this hardware assisted brute-force attack would take 61,973 years.
Even if we increased the brute force attack rate by a factor of a thousand, it would still take 62 years.
Brute force attacks, even fancy hardware-assisted brute force attacks, are still for dummies.
If this is the best your attackers can do, they're too stupid to be dangerous.
By the time you get to a mere 14 characters – even if they're all lowercase letters – you can pretty much forget about anyone brute forcing your password. Ever.
Hardware-assisted brute force attacks will never be a credible threat. But short, simple passwords are still dangerous.
It's much safer to think of your security in terms of passphrases instead of passwords.
key_insight: The exponential nature of password length makes even modest increases in character count far more effective than any hardware speedup, rendering brute force attacks fundamentally impractical against reasonably-sized passphrases.
